title: Out On A Limb

jokes.text: The narrator warns against ordering the Skip's Scramble immediately after describing it as famous and containing everything on the menu.
jokes.setup: The narrator describes the bistro's famous Skip's Scramble as an egg dish containing everything on the menu — building it up as a notable menu item.
jokes.punchline: Immediately, flatly: 'Don't order the Skip's Scramble.' No explanation given.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan warning subverts the build-up perfectly. The economy is exceptional — no explanation is funnier than any explanation would be. It also sets up a running gag for the entire episode and the 'On the Next' segment. Strong Arrested Development voice: the narrator as an unreliable/conspiratorial guide.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals that their eight dates included 'a record-tying three lovemaking sessions in one night for Michael,' punctuated by Michael's offscreen 'Yes!'
jokes.setup: Sally mentions how well things have been going since they started going out.
jokes.punchline: The narrator specifies the 'record-tying three lovemaking sessions in one night' as the defining metric of success, with Michael's triumphant 'Yes!' playing as a non-sequitur interjection.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'record-tying' is the key word — implying Michael has tracked this obsessively and that three is the record. The 'Yes!' lands as both a reaction beat and a setup for the running 'Yes!' gag that pays off later with Maggie. Character-specific: Michael's particular brand of dorky pride is perfectly captured. The rewatch bonus is high because 'Yes!' becomes significant retroactively.

jokes.text: Sally suggests they'd have had 'an entirely different kind of child' if they'd gotten together in high school, while Michael's actual son George Michael is shown nearby.
jokes.setup: Sally imagines an alternate life had they gotten together in high school, saying they'd have had a different kind of child.
jokes.punchline: The audience sees George Michael — an unusually earnest, awkward, nerdy teenager — implying Sally is inadvertently criticizing him.
jokes.explanation: Works as a gentle dig at George Michael's particular character type without being mean-spirited. The irony is that Sally doesn't know she's critiquing Michael's beloved son. Competent Arrested Development layering.

jokes.text: George Michael is nervous about being 'on his third Virgin Mary,' not realizing there's no alcohol in it.
jokes.setup: George Michael nervously mentions he's on his third Virgin Mary, seeming like someone who's had too much to drink.
jokes.punchline: Maeby points out there's no alcohol in a Virgin Mary. George Michael: 'There isn't? This is unbelievable.' Then immediately orders a virgin piña colada.
jokes.explanation: Nearly perfect character comedy. George Michael being nervous about drinking a non-alcoholic drink and then ordering another non-alcoholic drink 'to get things started' is a flawless comedic portrait of his sheltered, anxious character. The logic escalates absurdly: he's been tricking himself into being nervous for nothing, then doubles down. The line 'Now we'll get things started' after ordering a virgin piña colada is the perfect button. Could only be this character.

jokes.text: Michael's awkward aside 'She's a shot-putter. And I'm dating her.' after the kids leave the table.
jokes.setup: The kids have just met Sally and walked away.
jokes.punchline: Michael offers this unprompted non-sequitur, apparently feeling the need to justify or explain his girlfriend.
jokes.explanation: Works as a quick character beat — Michael's self-consciousness about his dating choices. The randomness of 'shot-putter' and the clipped delivery are funny. Doesn't rise to memorable but is a solid quick laugh.

jokes.text: Michael asks 'Who?' about Ann, then backtracks: 'I meant, uh, her.' — the running 'Ann? Her?' gag.
jokes.setup: George Michael suggests a double date with Ann. Michael asks 'Who?' — the show's recurring joke that no one can remember or care about Ann.
jokes.punchline: Michael catches himself and backpedals unconvincingly: 'Of course I know Ann. Yeah, see, I didn't mean who. I meant, uh, her. That's a great idea. I love Ann.'
jokes.explanation: The Ann running gag is one of Arrested Development's most beloved. This instance works because of the layering: Michael's 'Who?' is genuine, his recovery is clumsy, and 'I love Ann' is hilariously unconvincing. Rewatch bonus because fans anticipate and delight in each new iteration.

jokes.text: Ann confronts George Michael about skipping church to do 'neither' (not church, not working), then is horrified he's drinking — which is a Virgin Mary.
jokes.setup: Ann catches George Michael lying about working, not going to church, and apparently drinking.
jokes.punchline: The waiter arrives with his 'virgin piña colada,' making him look even more guilty of 'drinking' when he's entirely alcohol-free.
jokes.explanation: The callback lands perfectly — the piña colada arriving at exactly the wrong moment is structurally elegant. Ann's religious rigidity is character-specific comedy, and the absurdity of being caught 'drinking' non-alcoholic drinks pays off the earlier setup beautifully.

jokes.text: The waiter delivers George Michael's virgin piña colada mid-Ann's religious scolding, making it look like he's 'drinking'
jokes.setup: Ann is scolding George Michael for his Sunday sins — lying, not working, not going to church
jokes.punchline: The waiter arrives with 'Your virgin piña colada' at the exact moment Ann is mid-lecture, prompting her to gasp 'And now you're drinking?'
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed callback and visual gag combo. The virgin piña colada was set up earlier as a punchline about Sally not knowing there's no alcohol — now it arrives as a perfectly timed prop that makes George Michael look like a Sunday sinner. The word 'virgin' in this context landing in front of the religious Ann is also a quiet wordplay bonus. Strong structure, excellent economy.

jokes.text: George Michael on Ann: 'I mean, I guess we've lost some of the heat since we have kissed...I always assumed she would break up with me.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if George Michael is enjoying the relationship with Ann.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's bleak assessment: they've 'lost the heat' since kissing (implying kissing was the pinnacle), and his plan was always to wait for her to dump him.
jokes.explanation: Deeply character-specific. George Michael's romantic passivity — that kissing was the ceiling of their passion, and that he lacks the spine to break up with her himself — is a perfect portrait of his adolescent helplessness. 'I always assumed she would break up with me' is a quietly devastating line.

jokes.text: 'Well, she just killed my buzz.' — George Michael after Ann leaves
jokes.setup: Ann storms back to church after scolding everyone
jokes.punchline: George Michael's deadpan: 'Well, she just killed my buzz.' — funny because he's been drinking virgin drinks, so there was no buzz to kill
jokes.explanation: Compact and layered — George Michael using adult 'killed my buzz' language is funny given his age and goody-two-shoes nature, but it's doubly funny because his drinks were all non-alcoholic, so there was literally no buzz. Rewatch bonus for catching the virgin drinks callback.

jokes.text: Lindsay responds to Michael's news about three lovemaking sessions: 'Oh, big deal, three times. I've had three times plenty of times.'
jokes.setup: Michael is catching Lindsay up on his bond with Sally, implicitly referencing the three-times record.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's defensive 'big deal' response inadvertently reveals how rare it actually is for her — 'I've had three times plenty of times' is clumsy and unconvincing.
jokes.explanation: The repetition of 'three times' and 'plenty of times' in one sentence is grammatically awkward in exactly the right way, exposing Lindsay's actual deprivation while she's trying to dismiss the subject. Strong character integration — this is pure Lindsay.

jokes.text: 'Yes!' Michael's offscreen exclamation is followed by Lindsay asking 'Did you call my name?' / 'Absolutely not.'
jokes.setup: The 'Yes!' running gag — connected to Michael's three-times record — fires again at an inopportune moment.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay misinterprets 'Yes!' as Michael calling her name. Michael's flat denial: 'Absolutely not.'
jokes.explanation: The running gag is deployed with perfect economy. Lindsay's narcissistic interpretation of a random exclamation, and Michael's immediate flat denial, is both funny on its own and rewards viewers tracking the pattern. 'Absolutely not' is a great two-word button.

jokes.text: Lindsay says 'Oh, how delicious!' then 'Oh, hey, let's not spin in the comfy chair' then 'Look at this delicious piece of irony. I'm being sued for divorce.' — referring to Tobias being sued, not herself.
jokes.setup: Lindsay celebrates a 'delicious piece of irony' about being sued for divorce.
jokes.punchline: The irony is that Tobias (not Lindsay) is the one being sued, yet she savors it as entertainment rather than feeling any personal implication about their own marriage.
jokes.explanation: Wait — this is actually about Gob being sued for divorce by his wife. Lindsay enjoys the drama. The 'delicious' repetition and her detached amusement at marital strife (while in a dysfunctional marriage herself) is characteristic, but the joke doesn't quite snap.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Look at this delicious piece of irony. I'm being sued for divorce.'
jokes.setup: Lucille has just said not to spin in the comfy chair, then marvels at a letter
jokes.punchline: 'Look at this delicious piece of irony. I'm being sued for divorce.' — Lucille calls it ironic, but it's not ironic at all; she just likes the word
jokes.explanation: Lucille's misuse of 'irony' is a quick character beat — she uses the word because it sounds elevated, not because it applies. Also funny that she describes bad news as 'delicious.' Character-specific to Lucille's performative sophistication.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Yeah, I keep forgetting that I got married.' / Narrator: 'Gob met a woman one night. And after a series of escalating dares, married her.'
jokes.setup: Michael is confused that Gob is being sued for divorce when he thought Gob was single.
jokes.punchline: Gob casually admits he keeps forgetting he got married. The narrator explains it happened through 'escalating dares.'
jokes.explanation: 'A series of escalating dares' as the origin story of a marriage is a near-perfect piece of absurdist character comedy. It's entirely specific to Gob's impulsive, performative masculinity — he would absolutely accept any dare, including marriage. The casualness of 'I keep forgetting I got married' is also rich. Strong enough to justify 8+.

jokes.text: Gob's wife is revealed to be 'a seal dealer' — she 'raised and sold seals to aquariums.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks about the wife. Gob begins to explain.
jokes.punchline: 'She was a seal dealer' — an absurd profession delivered as though it's perfectly normal.
jokes.explanation: The show is planting a major foreshadowing element (the seal will bite off Buster's hand) while delivering it as pure comedy. 'Seal dealer' has a great sound, the detail is bizarre but internally logical, and it's delivered with the narrator's characteristic deadpan authority. Massive rewatch bonus once the ending is known.

jokes.text: Gob tried to include a seal in his magic act: 'There's a reason you don't typically see live seals in a magic show.'
jokes.setup: Gob explains that he's never been good at looking after seals, after being left with his wife's seals.
jokes.punchline: 'There's a reason you don't typically see live seals in a magic show' — delivered as hard-won wisdom, followed by the demonstration (barking, chaos).
jokes.explanation: Delivered like a proverb from someone who has made this specific mistake. The economy is excellent — one sentence conjures the entire catastrophic magic show. It's also planting the seal motif. The implied chaos of 'So with a simple wave of my—' [BARKING] is structurally perfect.

jokes.text: A seal interrupts Gob's magic trick attempt, causing a cat to screech. Gob's casual redirect: 'Oh, look-it over... there.'
jokes.setup: Gob is attempting to demonstrate a magic trick with 'a simple wave of my—'
jokes.punchline: A seal barks and disrupts everything, a cat screeches, and Gob resorts to his magician's deflection instinct: 'Oh, look-it over... there.'
jokes.explanation: Gob's magician's instinct — 'look over there' as a misdirection tool — deployed in a genuinely chaotic real-world situation is perfect character integration. His professional habits bleeding into life is a great recurring mode for the character.

jokes.text: Gob can't remember his wife's name. 'Krindy.' 'Name's not Krindy, Gob.' 'Ah, Saul Zentsman. No, that's her lawyer.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks for the wife's name quickly. Gob guesses.
jokes.punchline: He guesses wrong ('Krindy'), is corrected, then offers his lawyer's name ('Saul Zentsman') before catching himself.
jokes.explanation: The escalating wrongness — from a fake name to his wife's lawyer's name — perfectly captures Gob's breezy incompetence. 'Krindy' as a guess is itself funny (it's not even close to any real name). The structure of offering three wrong answers in rapid succession is tight and well-executed.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Well, she's got a name. And I'm gonna find out what it is, and then I'm gonna make a pun on it, and that's what I'll call her. Bad example — if her name's Amy, I'll call her Blamy.'
jokes.setup: Gob vows to discover his wife's name.
jokes.punchline: His plan is to make a pun on it. His demonstration of this plan: 'If her name's Amy, I'll call her Blamy.' — which is not a pun.
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's best jokes. The layers: Gob's vow to use her name against her is already pompous and absurd; his 'bad example' caveat actually makes it worse; and 'Blamy' is not a pun by any definition. Gob presenting this wordplay strategy with complete confidence while demonstrating that he doesn't understand what a pun is — and labeling his own bad example a 'bad example' — is brilliantly layered character comedy. Genuinely quotable and completely Gob-specific.

jokes.text: Michael suggests they take responsibility for their relationship failures. Lindsay: 'I think it's Mom's fault.' Michael: 'I was gonna say the exact same thing.'
jokes.setup: Michael tries to prompt self-reflection: 'Maybe it's time we actually took some responsibility for that.'
jokes.punchline: Both siblings immediately and unanimously blame their mother. Michael endorses Lindsay's deflection as his own intended answer.
jokes.explanation: The beat works because Michael's prompt toward responsibility is immediately and totally undermined by himself. His 'I was gonna say the exact same thing' is both a deflection and an admission. The Bluth family's collective inability to self-reflect is a thematic cornerstone, and this is an efficient execution of it.

jokes.text: Lucille rolls in on the spinning chair (which Lindsay had complained about) and delivers: 'So this is how my children speak of me when I'm not here.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is spinning in the comfy chair (Michael told her not to). They've just blamed their mother for everything.
jokes.punchline: Lucille arrives having apparently overheard everything, probably having just rolled in on the chair herself.
jokes.explanation: Classic sitcom timing of the eavesdropping character, but elevated by Lucille's imperious delivery and the grinding chair sound effect. The physical comedy of the spinning/grinding chair is implied. Good but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Lucille's response to Buster going to war: 'the answer is no, he's not going in the ocean.'
jokes.setup: Michael brings up that Buster wants to meet her at the beach and warns there are 'bigger dangers' than swimming — like having been signed up for the army.
jokes.punchline: Lucille dismisses the real danger (war) and insists on her own irrational fear: Buster going in the ocean.
jokes.explanation: A layered joke: (1) Lucille's denial of real danger by substituting a trivial one, (2) her controlling relationship with Buster, and (3) massive foreshadowing — it's not the ocean that gets him, it's a seal. Lucille's protectiveness manifesting as controlling delusion is perfectly in character. High rewatch value.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Like, say, signing him up for the army during wartime.' Lucille: 'What the hell was I supposed to do? Michael Moore confronted me in front of the whole country.'
jokes.setup: Michael points out the danger of Buster being in the army.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's justification: she was cornered by a Michael Moore look-alike on Jimmy Kimmel Live, which she frames as a nationally significant confrontation requiring decisive action.
jokes.explanation: The deflation of Lucille's self-importance: 'in front of the whole country' = a bit on Jimmy Kimmel Live. Michael's correction is perfectly calibrated. Lucille's grandiose victim narrative is character-specific and the absurdity of enlisting a son in a war as a response to a talk show stunt is very dark.

jokes.text: Michael corrects Lucille: 'First of all, it wasn't Michael Moore. It was a look-alike. And it wasn't in front of the whole country. It was for a bit on Jimmy Kimmel Live.' Lucille: 'I don't know who that is, and I don't care to find out.'
jokes.setup: Lucille has just invoked Michael Moore as justification for her actions.
jokes.punchline: Lucille dismisses Jimmy Kimmel's existence entirely, compounding the absurdity of her position with aristocratic disdain for pop culture.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's dismissal of Kimmel is perfectly in character — she only knows important people, and a late-night host isn't one of them. Paired with her confusion of a look-alike for the real Michael Moore, it paints a portrait of someone who experiences reality filtered through self-importance.

jokes.text: Lucille and Buster have hired a photographer for their beach meeting. 'We haven't done this since the Balboa Bay Window magazine.'
jokes.setup: A touching mother-son meeting at the beach, with a photographer present.
jokes.punchline: The photographer is there for a photo shoot of the two of them — as though they're celebrities or a romantic couple. Reference to previous magazine coverage.
jokes.explanation: The disturbing closeness of Buster and Lucille's relationship — treated so casually it includes professional photography — is characteristic dark humor. The Balboa Bay Window callback rewards dedicated viewers.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I was 30 years old then, Mother. I'm 32 now!' — correcting Lucille's claim about his age while explaining why he's going to war.
jokes.setup: Lucille is using 'Keepin' It Fresh' (a previous episode catchphrase or article) to dismiss Buster's concerns.
jokes.punchline: Buster's correction reveals he was 30 at the time of that reference, and is now 32 — highlighting how absurdly old he is to still be under his mother's thumb.
jokes.explanation: The age detail is the punchline — we're reminded that Buster is a 32-year-old man having this conversation with his mother about being 'allowed' to go to war. His indignation ('I'm 32 now!') over the principle rather than the content is tragicomic.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Well, I may not have been a perfect mother. But, you know, kids don't come with a handbook.' Narrator: 'In fact, there are thousands of books written on child-rearing. But Lucille was in denial.'
jokes.setup: Lucille offers the classic parental deflection: 'kids don't come with a handbook.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately fact-checks her: 'In fact, there are thousands of books written on child-rearing.'
jokes.explanation: A great use of the narrator as comedic weapon. The deadpan correction of Lucille's self-serving platitude with a simple, devastating fact is perfectly structured. The show's narrator as reality-checker against the Bluth family's denial is used here with maximum efficiency. The flat 'But Lucille was in denial' is the button.

jokes.text: Barry: 'A waitress that works there is claiming that by eating Skip's Scramble several times a week is making her a fatty.' — Barry's current case introduced.
jokes.setup: Barry explains the case he's currently working on.
jokes.punchline: The Skip's Scramble from the opening scene is now the subject of a lawsuit, and Barry uses the word 'fatty' in a legal context.
jokes.explanation: The Skip's Scramble running gag is being built out here — the dish is not just dangerous, it's legally actionable. The payoff of the opening narrator warning is satisfying. Barry's casual use of 'fatty' in a legal context also characterizes him perfectly as an incompetent lawyer.

jokes.text: Barry: 'Oh, well, I got Michael out of his marriage, didn't I?' Michael: 'Actually, she died.' Barry: 'You're kidding. I've been taking credit for that for years.' / 'Credit?'
jokes.setup: Barry brags about his legal track record.
jokes.punchline: He's been wrongly taking credit for a woman's death as a professional achievement. Michael's horrified 'Credit?' is the button.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark joke. Barry has been professionally bragging for years about an outcome he had nothing to do with — a client's wife dying — as though he engineered it. The casual revelation, his complete lack of remorse, and Michael's single horrified 'Credit?' make this a standout moment. Barry is defined in one exchange: incompetent, self-aggrandizing, and morally empty.

jokes.text: Barry about opposing counsel: 'I have Chinese with him every Friday night at Miss Temple's.' Then: 'He's tough.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Barry knows the opposing lawyer Saul Zentsman.
jokes.punchline: Barry describes a personal dining relationship with opposing counsel as the primary fact about him, then adds 'He's tough' as an afterthought — suggesting the social relationship is his main concern.
jokes.explanation: Barry's tangled social and professional life is a recurring theme. The Miss Temple's reference (a gay venue, given the show's Barry running gags) adds a layer for attentive viewers. Competent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I just want my wife back.' Barry: 'Save it for the stand, okay, Tom Jane?'
jokes.setup: Gob makes a genuine emotional declaration to his lawyer.
jokes.punchline: Barry dismisses it as performative and misnames him 'Tom Jane' (not his name).
jokes.explanation: The misname is a quick character beat for Barry — he can't be bothered to learn his client's name. 'Save it for the stand' treating sincere emotion as tactical material is also darkly funny. Works quickly.

jokes.text: Barry: 'You know who's defending the fatty? Maggie Lizer.' — introducing Maggie as the opposing counsel in the fat suit case.
jokes.setup: Barry is describing the Skip's Scramble lawsuit.
jokes.punchline: The opposing counsel is Maggie Lizer — Michael's ex who is actually faking a pregnancy, which will intersect with the 'fatty' plot in a deeply structured way.
jokes.explanation: This is structural comedy — the plot threads are being woven together in a way that rewards attentive viewers. The 'fatty' case, the Skip's Scramble, and Maggie's fake pregnancy are all colliding. On rewatch, the elegance of the construction is even more impressive.

jokes.text: Narrator explains Maggie was 'both pretending to be blind and prosecuting the family' during her affair with Michael. 'It was with her he'd first set the three-time record. Yes!'
jokes.setup: Narrator provides backstory on Maggie Lizer.
jokes.punchline: The three-time record callback fires again with another 'Yes!' — now tied to Maggie specifically.
jokes.explanation: By the third deployment, the 'Yes!' has become a signature callback the audience anticipates. The fact that it's now revealed as tied to Maggie — the woman who may have fathered his child — retroactively adds irony to every previous 'Yes!' The narrator's deadpan delivery makes it sing.

jokes.text: Barry: 'Tell you the truth, I preferred it when she was blind.' — said about opposing counsel Maggie Lizer.
jokes.setup: Barry is observing Maggie's effectiveness in court.
jokes.punchline: Barry's preferred tactical position was when opposing counsel was pretending to be blind. He says this with no irony.
jokes.explanation: Extremely quotable, pitch-perfect. Barry's preference for fighting a disabled opponent is casually monstrous and entirely in character. The line does maximum work in eight words: characterizes Barry, references the show's mythology, and gets a huge laugh through pure economy and dark confidence.

jokes.text: Maggie's client (the 'fatty') wins over the jury by being visibly sympathetic. Barry: 'Oh, I'm cooked. They love her.'
jokes.setup: Barry watches Maggie parade the 'fat' plaintiff before the jury.
jokes.punchline: Barry despairs. (The irony that the plaintiff is wearing a fat suit will pay off later.)
jokes.explanation: Functional moment that sets up the fat suit reveal. Barry's incompetent despair is in character. Gets a mild laugh on first watch; on rewatch the irony of the jury loving a fake fat plaintiff is richer.

jokes.text: Maggie: 'To tell you the truth, I am so relieved not to be pretending to be blind anymore. It's actually taught me not to rely on cheap tricks to get the jury's sympathy.'
jokes.setup: Maggie catches up with Michael outside the courtroom, expressing relief at no longer faking blindness.
jokes.punchline: She credits this experience with teaching her not to use cheap tricks — while she is actively wearing a fake pregnancy belly to gain jury sympathy.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest jokes. The irony is perfectly layered: Maggie earnestly credits her fake blindness with teaching her not to fake disabilities for sympathy — while she is currently faking a pregnancy for sympathy. The audience knows both things simultaneously. The line is beautifully written because it works as a sincere statement AND as a devastating self-own. High rewatch value because you can see it coming and it still lands.

jokes.text: A juror has knitted something for Maggie during the trial. Maggie: 'Oh, Juror Number Four!' / 'I feel so bad I fought so hard to keep you off the jury.'
jokes.setup: Maggie has cultivated such sympathy from the jury that a juror knitted her a gift.
jokes.punchline: Maggie expresses regret at having tried to block this particular juror — the implication being she would've wanted even more of this kind of jury manipulation. The judge intervenes.
jokes.explanation: The scale of jury manipulation implied is darkly funny. The knitting gift is absurdist (who knits for a plaintiff's attorney during a trial?), and Maggie's genuine regret at having excluded such a sympathetic juror is a good character beat.

jokes.text: The judge has his bailiff assemble an 'Ocean-Sounds Toddler Rocker' as a gift for Maggie's baby — in chambers.
jokes.setup: Judge Ping summons Maggie to chambers.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals the judge has also been manipulated by Maggie's fake pregnancy into giving her a baby gift — assembled by his bailiff.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of Maggie's manipulation — from jury to judge — is structurally satisfying. 'Ocean-Sounds Toddler Rocker he'd asked his bailiff to assemble' is a great specific detail. The specificity of the gift name is very Arrested Development.

jokes.text: Michael to Maggie about the knitted item: 'Too bad the trial's not going longer. She could've knit you something that fit.'
jokes.setup: Michael and Maggie are chatting; the juror has knitted something too small for Maggie's (fake) pregnant belly.
jokes.punchline: Michael's joke implies the knitted item doesn't fit because Maggie is so large — he's complimenting her 'pregnancy' inadvertently. He backtracks with 'They look great.'
jokes.explanation: Michael's awkward backpedal ('No, no, come on. They look great. You look great. You look fantastic.') is the joke — his social incompetence when faced with a potentially pregnant woman. Competent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Maggie says it's been 'about eight and a half months' since Michael saw her. Michael: 'Eight and a half months, huh? Wow. I see somebody has been counting the, uh—' [trails off in realization]
jokes.setup: Michael tries to figure out when they last met. Maggie corrects his estimate to 'about eight and a half months.'
jokes.punchline: Michael starts a flirtatious quip about her counting the days — then the number hits him. Eight and a half months. She is eight and a half months pregnant. He trails off.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured: the misdirection works because Michael (and the audience) follows him into flirtation, then both are interrupted mid-sentence by arithmetic. The unfinished sentence is the punchline — what he was going to say becomes irrelevant as reality sets in. The repetition ('Eight and a half months. Wow. Really? Could've sworn it was a year.') captures his desperate mental processing. Excellent structure.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Michael had just learned that the woman he'd been intimate with eight and a half months earlier was eight-and-a-half months pregnant.'
jokes.setup: Michael has been slowly computing the math of Maggie's pregnancy timing.
jokes.punchline: The narrator states the obvious conclusion with clinical precision, refusing to editorialize.
jokes.explanation: The narrator stating the obvious in the driest possible terms is a classic AD move. The repetition of 'eight and a half months' three times in close succession — from Maggie, from Michael, from the narrator — is a comedic drum roll. The economy of the narrator's formulation is excellent.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Didn't I use something?' Maggie: 'No.' Michael: 'You thought I was blind, remember?'
jokes.setup: Michael panics about the pregnancy and wonders about contraception.
jokes.punchline: Maggie reminds him that he thought she was blind — implying he skipped contraception because he assumed she couldn't see what he was doing (or for some other bizarre logic specific to his naivety).
jokes.explanation: The implication — that Michael didn't use contraception because he thought she was blind — is absurdist logic that somehow tracks with the show's world. It's dark, specific, and deeply weird in the best way. 'You thought I was blind, remember?' as a matter-of-fact explanation of contraceptive negligence is a great line. The character integration is high because this is specifically about Michael's naivety and the show's mythology.

jokes.text: Maggie: 'Don't worry. You're not the father, Michael.' / Michael exhales in relief / then immediately: 'No problem. Really? Right. You sure? I mean, uh— I could be. What if I was? Don't you want to discuss this maybe?'
jokes.setup: Maggie reassures Michael he's not the father.
jokes.punchline: Michael's relief immediately reverses into insistence that maybe he could be the father — a perfect portrait of his inability to process good news.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully captures Michael's self-defeating psychology. He gets what he wants, takes one beat to enjoy it, then talks himself back into the problem. 'I could be. What if I was?' after exhaling in relief is a perfect reversal. The character integration is exceptional — Michael's compulsion to complicate his own life is a defining trait.

jokes.text: Maggie: 'If you're interested, come to the baptism. You can hurl a Bible at the baby.'
jokes.setup: Michael is pushing to discuss the paternity. Maggie shuts it down.
jokes.punchline: Her dismissal is framed as a casual invitation to commit infant assault.
jokes.explanation: Extremely quotable. The specific violence of 'hurl a Bible at the baby' — the choice of Bible being perfect given the baptism context — and the casual invitation tone make this a standout one-liner. Economy is near-perfect. Maggie's character is established in one sharp line.

jokes.text: Michael explains to Sally that Maggie said it wasn't his baby — 'She said it wasn't mine, so—' Sally: 'Why are you telling me this?'
jokes.setup: Michael is explaining the Maggie situation to Sally.
jokes.punchline: Michael reveals his reasoning: because Maggie (a known liar) said it's NOT his, he believes it IS his. Sally's 'That's a great way to say that. We really do communicate well, do you know that?' is the button.
jokes.explanation: Sally's summary — 'you think you're having a baby because a woman who lies told you you weren't' — is a sharp compression of Michael's convoluted logic. His response ('That's a great way to say that') is adorably oblivious. Good exchange.

jokes.text: Lindsay summarizes Michael's love life: 'Are you insane? You broke up with her because somebody who doesn't want you might be pregnant with your baby?'
jokes.setup: Michael explains the situation to Lindsay.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's compression of the situation is brutal and accurate, and Michael is forced to correct technical details ('Sally broke up with me').
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's occasional sharp reads on Michael's behavior are funny because she's usually self-absorbed. When she nails him, it lands harder. Her priorities remain self-oriented even while being insightful.

jokes.text: Michael says 'All I could see was her giant chest— box. She had this— Some people call it a chest. I call it a box.'
jokes.setup: Michael is explaining that he didn't realize Maggie was pregnant because he saw her carrying a 'giant chest.'
jokes.punchline: He catches himself on 'chest' and pivots to 'box' — a wooden box — while making his original meaning (chest/cleavage) the subtext the audience hears.
jokes.explanation: Michael's stumbling correction of 'chest' to 'box' is both a character beat (his awkwardness around women) and a good wordplay moment. The audience fills in what 'chest' originally referred to. Works as an in-the-moment fumble.

jokes.text: Gob's solution to the pregnancy mystery: 'Make 'em pee in a cup, and pee in a cup right in front of me.' / Lindsay: '...A pregnancy test.' Gob: 'Oh! [laughs] There's something we never had, huh, Lindsay?'
jokes.setup: The family is brainstorming how to verify Maggie's pregnancy. Gob proposes his urine-collection surveillance scheme.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay realizes he's describing a pregnancy test. Gob has an 'Oh!' moment — then pivots to a dark joke about Tobias and Lindsay's inability to conceive naturally.
jokes.explanation: Gob reinventing the pregnancy test through incompetence is funny on its own. The pivot to 'something we never had, huh, Lindsay?' — meaning a pregnancy test because they couldn't conceive naturally — is darker and sharper. Two-stage joke with escalating impact.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'There's something we never had, huh, Lindsay? Oh, no.' — about a pregnancy test
jokes.setup: Michael realizes a pregnancy test solves his problem; Tobias hears 'pregnancy test'
jokes.punchline: Tobias: 'There's something we never had, huh, Lindsay? Oh, no.' — catching himself with 'Oh, no' as he processes that the absence of a positive pregnancy test in their marriage means something
jokes.explanation: The 'Oh, no.' self-correction is the joke — Tobias starts to be nostalgic/wistful and then processes the implication mid-sentence. Combined with the backstory that Maeby was adopted via 'science and money,' this lands hard. Strong Tobias character integration.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'We had to create our little Frankenstein monster out of science and money and just a dash of—' [notices Maeby] 'Maeby, what— h-how long have you been standing there?'
jokes.setup: Tobias is waxing poetic about Maeby's unusual conception.
jokes.punchline: He catches himself mid-phrase ('just a dash of—') when he realizes Maeby is right there. The unfinished sentence is the punchline — what was 'a dash of' that he can't say in front of her?
jokes.explanation: The incomplete sentence is the joke — the audience imagines the worst, and Tobias's horror at being overheard validates that imagination. 'Frankenstein monster' as a description of Maeby is also quietly mean in a way that's characteristic of Tobias's oblivious cruelty.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Seems like only yesterday you were bursting forth from your mother's fertile womb.'
jokes.setup: Maeby says she 'just walked in'; Tobias is caught having described her as a Frankenstein monster
jokes.punchline: Tobias recovers by sentimentally saying 'Seems like only yesterday you were bursting forth from your mother's fertile womb' — which is (a) untrue since she's adopted, (b) Tobias's characteristically clinical-yet-overwrought language
jokes.explanation: Tobias's pseudo-therapeutic, pseudo-theatrical vocabulary ('bursting forth from your mother's fertile womb') is perfectly him, and doubly funny because Maeby didn't burst forth from Lindsay's womb at all. The recovery attempt makes things worse, which is vintage Tobias.

jokes.text: Lindsay and Tobias agree to break into Maggie's house to collect a urine specimen. Lindsay: 'We will be like Tracy and Hepburn.' Tobias bursts in: 'What do you say, you old poop?' Michael: 'Oh, God, he's Hepburn.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay invokes 'Tracy and Hepburn' as their adventurous couple dynamic.
jokes.punchline: Tobias enters doing a Katharine Hepburn impression, confirming that in their dynamic, Tobias is the woman — a recurring theme about Tobias's sexuality.
jokes.explanation: Perfect deployment of the Tobias sexuality running gag. The Tracy and Hepburn setup is immediately confirmed as having the genders reversed. 'What do you say, you old poop?' as Hepburn is both a good impression gag and a character revelation. Michael's 'Oh, God, he's Hepburn' is a perfect button — knowing, resigned, and specific.

jokes.text: Lucille declares 'I am done with Buster. He pushes my buttons.' Michael responds: 'He also zips you up and sands your elbows.'
jokes.setup: Lucille complains about Buster 'pushing her buttons.'
jokes.punchline: Michael lists the other things Buster actually does for Lucille — 'zips you up and sands your elbows' — revealing the extent of her dependency on him for personal grooming.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Sands your elbows' is the key — it's specific enough to be shocking, implies an entire world of inappropriate mother-son dependency, and is completely unexpected. 'Zips you up' might be explained away, but 'sands your elbows' is bizarre personal grooming that no adult should be doing for another adult. The specificity is what makes it exceptional. Highly quotable, strong character integration — this is the Buster/Lucille relationship in one line.

jokes.text: Lucille claims Buster is 'pretending he's going to war. Mr. Big Shot.' / Michael: 'Wait a minute. He said he's going to war? Aren't you worried?'
jokes.setup: Lucille dismisses Buster's war deployment as attention-seeking.
jokes.punchline: Her framing of a genuine military deployment as 'Mr. Big Shot' behavior is comically delusional. Michael's genuine concern highlights how unhinged her reaction is.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's denial continuing to manifest as narcissistic interpretation of Buster's behavior. Competent character comedy but not a standout moment.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'I need macaroons.' Michael: 'Oh, you're high!' / Lucille: 'Oh, well, you can win every argument like that, but that does not make you right.'
jokes.setup: Lucille requests macaroons at a moment of emotional stress.
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately diagnoses drug use. Lucille's retort — 'you can win every argument like that, but that does not make you right' — is both defensive and inadvertently confirmatory.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's defense — that she's not being proven wrong just because the accusation is accurate — is a rich piece of character comedy. Her response is simultaneously a legal-ish argument and an admission. The delivery would make it land even harder. Strong Lucille moment.

jokes.text: Lucille shouts 'I don't want you telling him you're his father! I need ice!' in the same breath — apparent non-sequitur reveals Oscar may be Buster's father.
jokes.setup: Michael and Lucille are arguing about Buster and denial.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's fragmented outburst — 'I don't want you telling him you're his father! I need ice!' — reveals a major plot secret (Oscar is Buster's father) while treating it as equivalent in urgency to needing ice.
jokes.explanation: The major plot revelation delivered mid-argument, immediately followed by 'I need ice,' is structurally brilliant. The comedy comes from the disproportion: a secret that upends Buster's entire identity is sandwiched between two mundane requests. Classic Arrested Development: the biggest revelations are treated with the least ceremony. High rewatch value because first-time viewers may miss what was just revealed.

jokes.text: 'You're high!' / 'You're drunk!' — Michael and Lucille argue back and forth as the pipe grinds.
jokes.setup: The argument escalates with both parties accusing each other of being intoxicated.
jokes.punchline: Both accusations are probably true (Lucille is on pot, Michael may have been drinking), making the fight a standoff between two impaired parties.
jokes.explanation: Functional exchange that characterizes both characters. The symmetry of the insults is quick and efficient. Not exceptional but earns its place.

jokes.text: Tobias diagnoses Lucille with 'a psychological concept known as denial' — then Lucille: 'You are a worse psychiatrist than you are a son-in-law, and you will never get work as an actor because you have no talent.'
jokes.setup: Tobias attempts to intervene with psychological insight.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's three-part destruction of Tobias — psychiatrist, son-in-law, actor — is precise and devastating, hitting all of his failures at once.
jokes.explanation: Lucille demolishing Tobias on three fronts simultaneously — and being completely correct on all three — is a great character moment for both of them. Tobias's dignified response ('Well, if she's not going to say anything, I certainly can't help her') is the perfect button: he retreats into professional detachment as though he wasn't just destroyed. Both characters are defined in four lines.

jokes.text: Lucille's takedown of Tobias: 'You are a worse psychiatrist than you are a son-in-law, and you will never get work as an actor because you have no talent.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has just attempted to diagnose Lucille with denial using condescending clinical language
jokes.punchline: Lucille delivers a triple-pronged attack: worst psychiatrist, worst son-in-law, will never act because he has no talent — then Tobias: 'Well, if she's not going to say anything, I certainly can't help her.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Lucille's attack is devastating because it's true on all three counts, delivered with perfect queenly brutality. But the real punchline is Tobias's response: 'Well, if she's not going to say anything, I certainly can't help her.' — he interprets Lucille's scorched-earth attack as 'not saying anything,' revealing his total lack of self-awareness and the depth of his delusion about his psychiatric abilities. The double structure (Lucille's attack + Tobias's oblivious response) is what elevates this to the episode's peak.

jokes.text: Tobias's response to Lucille's devastating takedown: 'Well, if she's not going to say anything, I certainly can't help her.'
jokes.setup: Lucille has just devastated Tobias on every professional and personal front.
jokes.punchline: Tobias retreats into professional therapist posture as though the personal attack didn't land, framing her response as therapeutic non-cooperation rather than accurate criticism.
jokes.explanation: This is arguably funnier than Lucille's line because of what it reveals about Tobias's psychology. He processes a personal destruction through his professional framework — Lucille isn't attacking him, she's a patient in denial. His self-delusion is so complete that he makes himself the victim without trying. 'Certainly can't help her' with professional resignation is a brilliant character-specific deflection. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'I know how to get rid of Ann. You put her in a room with Gangy. She'll criticize her away.' George Michael: 'But what would Gangy have to criticize about Ann?'
jokes.setup: Maeby proposes using Lucille as a weapon against Ann.
jokes.punchline: George Michael naively asks what Lucille could possibly criticize about Ann — not understanding that Lucille can criticize anything. The narrator or Maeby's expression presumably answers: everything.
jokes.explanation: George Michael's innocent faith in Ann combined with his total ignorance of Lucille's all-encompassing critical capacity is character-specific. The joke works because the audience knows the answer to his question, and Maeby knows, and the gap between their knowledge and his is the comedy.

jokes.text: George Sr. is wearing the 'God costume' from the annual Living Classics Pageant — a recreation of Michelangelo's Creation of Adam where he plays God.
jokes.setup: Michael enters the attic to find George Sr. in costume.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals this is the God costume used at the 'annual Living Classics Pageant where he and Buster re-created Michelangelo's Creation of Adam.'
jokes.explanation: The image of George Sr. in a God costume in the attic is inherently funny. The narrator's explanation — that he and Buster annually recreated the Creation of Adam together — is rich with implication about their relationship and George Sr.'s grandiosity. The detail is absurd enough to be memorable.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'What if I never get a chance to reach out and touch that hand of his again?' — in God costume, referencing the Creation of Adam pose.
jokes.setup: George Sr. expresses fear of never seeing Buster again before he goes to war.
jokes.punchline: His expression of paternal love is filtered through the Creation of Adam imagery — reaching out to touch Buster's hand as God reaches for Adam. The grandiosity of his self-image (as God) while genuinely worrying is darkly comic.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the costume — genuine paternal emotion expressed while dressed as God, with the Creation of Adam as the emotional template. The absurdity of the visual grounds what could be a sincere moment.

jokes.text: Michael catches George Sr. leaving the attic to visit the Ford dealership; George Sr. defends it: 'Have you seen the new Ford Mustang? You could hump that hood.'
jokes.setup: Michael chides his father for not staying in the attic — a fugitive from justice
jokes.punchline: 'Yeah, well, have you seen the new Ford Mustang? You could hump that hood.' — George Sr.'s defense of risking arrest is his enthusiasm for a car
jokes.explanation: 'You could hump that hood' is a memorably crude and enthusiastic line that reveals George Sr.'s complete inability to prioritize correctly (fugitive from justice vs. car appreciation). The specificity of the Mustang and the vulgarity of 'hump that hood' make this quotable.

jokes.text: George Sr. claims he has 'never left the attic.' Michael: 'Two days ago. Right? Saw you floating around the Ford dealership.' George Sr.: 'Yeah, well, have you seen the new Ford Mustang? You could hump that hood.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. protests that he can't leave the attic as a wanted man.
jokes.punchline: He immediately concedes to a recent trip to a Ford dealership, and his justification — 'You could hump that hood' — is delivered as though this is a reasonable reason to risk federal fugitive status.
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s fugitive lifestyle being interrupted by consumer enthusiasm for a new car is absurdly funny. 'You could hump that hood' is extremely quotable — the casualness and completeness of his justification (no follow-up defense, just this statement) implies he believes this absolutely. Exceptional economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Maggie is 'practicing with the pizza guy' — rehearsing how to answer the door for a pizza delivery to fool someone into thinking she's not home.
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at Maggie's door. She answers with 'Are you blind again? For the pizza guy. I give him a five. I tell him to keep the change.'
jokes.punchline: Maggie is running drills with a fake pizza delivery to practice pretending she's blind — for what purpose is unclear, revealing her habitual deception.
jokes.explanation: The blind pizza routine is a quick absurdist gag that characterizes Maggie's compulsive deceptiveness. Even when she's supposedly not faking it, she's faking it. Decent joke, slightly underdeveloped.

jokes.text: Maggie: 'She's taking the stand tomorrow. I want to make sure it creaks.' — about the fake-fat plaintiff
jokes.setup: Michael is stalling Maggie while Lindsay and Tobias break into her house; Maggie is coaching a witness
jokes.punchline: 'She's taking the stand tomorrow. I want to make sure it creaks.' — implying the plaintiff is heavy enough that the witness stand will creak under her weight, which Maggie wants as a theatrical effect
jokes.explanation: A dark and funny line — Maggie is not just coaching the witness but engineering the physical environment for maximum jury sympathy. 'I want to make sure it creaks' is beautifully specific and reveals Maggie's manipulative nature. Rewatch bonus because we later learn the plaintiff is wearing a fat suit, making the creak engineering even more absurd.

jokes.text: Michael: 'There's so much I don't understand about the law.' — said while stalling Maggie so his family can break into her house.
jokes.setup: Michael is making small talk to keep Maggie occupied.
jokes.punchline: His comment about not understanding the law is a deliberate non-sequitur stall, but it's also true, and it's being said to a lawyer while he facilitates a break-in.
jokes.explanation: Quick ironic moment — Michael literally doing something illegal while claiming legal ignorance. Works as a beat but doesn't do heavy lifting.

jokes.text: Lindsay 'cloaks herself in Maggie's scent' using Maggie's perfume because 'she was blind when she bought this.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay and Tobias are sneaking into Maggie's house. Lindsay picks up perfume and sprays it on.
jokes.punchline: Tobias points out Maggie isn't blind. Lindsay: 'Well, she was when she bought this.' — the logic being that perfume chosen while blind is bad perfume, so smelling like it is good camouflage.
jokes.explanation: The logic chain — blind person → bad perfume → use as disguise scent — is absurdist but internally consistent. 'Well, she was when she bought this' is a great non-sequitur that somehow makes perfect sense in the moment. Lindsay's complete commitment to terrible logic is character-specific.

jokes.text: Buster's response to the paternity reveal: 'You lied to me. Uncle Oscar's my father. He just told me so. And maybe you're not even my real mother. For all I know, Lucille 2 is my real mother.'
jokes.setup: Buster has just learned Oscar is his father and is confronting Lucille.
jokes.punchline: His logic spirals: if one parent lied, maybe the other isn't real either. He lands on Lucille 2 as an alternative mother — escalating from a reasonable grievance to an absurd conspiracy.
jokes.explanation: Buster's emotional breakdown taking the form of expanding conspiracy theories is in character — his trauma expresses itself as an inability to trust any foundational fact. 'Lucille 2 is my real mother' is absurd but follows from his escalating logic, and lands as a great button.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Well, here's something you should know. I'm going to war!' — shouted triumphantly as an act of rebellion.
jokes.setup: Lucille tries to manage the paternity fallout.
jokes.punchline: Buster's ultimate act of defiance is... following through on the war he was already being deployed to. His 'rebellion' is involuntary compliance.
jokes.explanation: Buster's 'rebellion' being something he has no real control over is tragicomic. His triumphant declaration of something happening to him (not chosen by him) as though it's an act of will is perfectly Buster.

jokes.text: Lindsay and Tobias in Maggie's shower: 'That's the first time we were in the shower since our honeymoon. And this time, no tears.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay and Tobias are hiding in Maggie's shower after nearly being caught.
jokes.punchline: Tobias treats this as a romantic milestone in their marriage — and the qualifier 'this time, no tears' implies their honeymoon shower was tearful (implying the horror of their wedding night).
jokes.explanation: 'No tears' is doing enormous work in two words. It implies their honeymoon shower was miserable, their marriage has been barren of intimacy, and Tobias's bar for romantic success is so low that an emergency hide in a stranger's shower counts as progress. Character integration is exceptional — this is a precise portrait of their marriage and Tobias's sexuality. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Michael about Sally: 'Yeah, I know. It's a funny name. But she's a doll.' Maggie: 'Yeah, I had one.' / 'If you pull her hair, she'd cry or something.' Michael: 'This one kinda does that too.'
jokes.setup: Michael is talking about Sally Sitwell with Maggie. Maggie says she had a Sally Sitwell doll.
jokes.punchline: Michael confirms that the real Sally shares qualities with the doll: 'If you pull her hair, she'd cry.' 'This one kinda does that too.' — Michael inadvertently characterizing his girlfriend as childlike or fragile.
jokes.explanation: The Sally Sitwell doll is a nice absurdist touch (the Sitwells apparently had toy dolls made in their likeness). Michael's confirmation of the doll's properties applying to the real Sally is a quick dark character beat.

jokes.text: Michael: 'If you pull her hair, she'd cry or something. This one kinda does that too.' — about Sally Sitwell
jokes.setup: Discussing Sally's name sounding like a doll ('she's a doll')
jokes.punchline: Michael adds that the real Sally 'kinda does that too' when you pull her hair — a casually inappropriate thing to say about your girlfriend to your pregnant ex
jokes.explanation: A mildly funny extension of the doll joke — Michael inadvertently sharing something odd about his current girlfriend's behavior while talking to his ex. Decent but not a standout.

jokes.text: Lindsay on the phone from Maggie's house: 'Listen, you should know that that box that says 60 pounds — it's actually like two pounds. I think she's pregnant, Michael.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has found the 'heavy' box Michael referenced. It says 60 pounds but is extremely light.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay concludes from the baby items inside that Maggie IS pregnant — the opposite of the truth (the box contains the fat suit/fake pregnancy apparatus).
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony is dense here — Lindsay is finding evidence that she reads as confirming pregnancy while it actually proves the fake. The box saying '60 pounds' but weighing two pounds plants the fake pregnancy reveal. Good structural comedy that rewards rewatch.

jokes.text: Michael to Maggie: 'So, no more lies. Tell me the truth. If that baby is mine, maybe we're meant to be together.' Maggie: 'Of course it is, Michael.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks for honesty about the pregnancy.
jokes.punchline: Maggie immediately lies in response to being asked to 'tell the truth' — confirming paternity to get what she wants.
jokes.explanation: The irony of Maggie confirming the lie when asked for truth is well-structured. Works as a character-specific moment — this is exactly what Maggie does.

jokes.text: The Fünkes discover the fake pregnant belly/fat suit in the mystery box — a prosthetic stomach.
jokes.setup: Lindsay and Tobias have been trying to determine whether Maggie is really pregnant. They find the box that should weigh 60 pounds.
jokes.punchline: Inside is a fake pregnant belly — simultaneously solving the pregnancy mystery and the 'fatty' lawsuit mystery.
jokes.explanation: This is where all the episode's threads converge — the fat suit serves dual purpose as fake pregnancy belly and fake 'fatty' plaintiff apparatus. The structural payoff of the Skip's Scramble / fatty lawsuit / fake pregnancy is extremely satisfying. On rewatch, all the plants are visible.

jokes.text: Lucille's attempt to have Ann criticized away by Lucille fails: Lucille is too distracted by Buster's departure to criticize. Ann: 'Hey, do you have any bread pudding? 'Cause you'd be surprised how much this one can put away.'
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby bring Ann to Lucille hoping Lucille will drive her away.
jokes.punchline: Lucille is too emotionally devastated to criticize. Ann — supposedly the meek one — casually asks for bread pudding and invites Lucille to notice how much she eats.
jokes.explanation: The reversal is the joke — the plan fails not because Lucille is kind, but because she's too distracted. Ann's unexpected assertion ('you'd be surprised how much this one can put away') reveals a more self-aware and even slightly aggressive Ann beneath the meek surface. Subverts expectations neatly.

jokes.text: Ann tells the grief-stricken Lucille: 'You know, when times are tough, you can always turn to the power of prayer.' George Michael: 'Here we go.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is visibly devastated. Ann offers comfort.
jokes.punchline: Ann's instinct is to pray. George Michael's 'Here we go' shows his resigned familiarity with this — he knows exactly where this leads and he's exhausted by it.
jokes.explanation: George Michael's 'Here we go' is the joke — his worn-down acceptance of Ann's one-note religious response to everything. The fact that he dates her despite this exasperation is the subtext. Economy is very good.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'This was a big get for God.' — on Ann converting the grief-stricken Lucille to prayer.
jokes.setup: Ann has invited Lucille to pray. Lucille, in her emotional state, agrees.
jokes.punchline: The narrator frames God gaining a convert as a sports transaction: 'This was a big get for God.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. Using sports/business acquisition language ('a big get') to describe a divine event is perfectly unexpected. The economy is exceptional — five words reframe God as a franchise making roster moves. The narrator's deadpan delivery makes it land harder. It's also darkly funny because of what it implies about God's typical clientele vs. Lucille Bluth. Very quotable, completely original.

jokes.text: Lindsay's recorded message plays on Michael's phone: 'Hey, so what's the deal on Zuckerkorn? 'Cause you know my fat case — it's really pretty thin.' / 'Lying, untrustworthy—' 'Yeah.' 'Plus, I heard he hangs out at rest stops.'
jokes.setup: Michael's phone plays a recorded message from Lindsay at exactly the wrong moment — while he's with Maggie.
jokes.punchline: The message describes someone as 'lying, untrustworthy' — Michael initially thinks it's about him, but it's about Zuckerkorn. Then Lindsay says he 'hangs out at rest stops' — a gay cruising reference for Barry.
jokes.explanation: The accidental phone playback is a classic comic device, but it's elevated by the content: 'lying, untrustworthy' applying equally to Maggie (in the room) and Zuckerkorn, and the 'fat case — it's really pretty thin' pun is an elegant callback to both plots. The rest stop reference is a good Barry running gag deployment.

jokes.text: Michael confronts Maggie with the fat suit: 'Which one are you wearing right now? Is this the ready-to-burst model?'
jokes.setup: Michael has just been told by his sister that there's a fake pregnant belly in Maggie's house.
jokes.punchline: Michael's confrontation is contemptuous and specific: 'What've you got? A different one for each trimester?' and 'Is this the ready-to-burst model?'
jokes.explanation: 'A different one for each trimester' and 'the ready-to-burst model' are both good lines — imagining a catalog of fake pregnancy bellies is a funny absurdist extension. Michael's anger expressing itself through consumer product specifics is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Michael breaks up with Maggie: 'Good-bye. It's been fun—' [pause] '—not.'
jokes.setup: Michael tries to deliver a cutting exit line.
jokes.punchline: He lands on '—not,' which the narrator immediately comments on: 'Michael had hoped something more dramatic would have come out of his mouth. Or at least more current.'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-comedic moment. Michael deploys an embarrassingly dated insult ('not!') and the narrator immediately editorializes on his behalf — capturing the gap between Michael's self-image and his actual social grace. 'Or at least more current' is devastating. The structure (pause before '—not') and the narrator's response are perfectly timed. The joke works because it characterizes Michael (dorky, awkward even in his righteous moments) and also makes fun of the trope itself.

jokes.text: Maggie's riposte: 'Oh, you're so right, Michael. I really can't be trusted.' [pause] 'Cause I'm fakin' it—' [pause] '—not.'
jokes.setup: Maggie responds to Michael's 'not' exit line.
jokes.punchline: She uses the same '—not' construction to imply she IS actually pregnant (i.e., she's not faking it). But she is faking it — so the 'not' here implies a double negative that is confusing enough to be funny.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Michael's exact construction, used against him, is clever and fast. The double-negative of 'faking it — not' implies she's not faking the pregnancy, but she IS — so the 'not' actually makes her statement true, then false. It's also funnier that Maggie, a better verbal combatant than Michael, can deploy his own terrible 90s slang more effectively. Maggie is established as sharper in two words.

jokes.text: Tobias arrives to deliver the pregnancy confirmation: 'My wife and I finally had the thrill of seeing a plus sign on a pregnancy test. You're gonna be a daddy!'
jokes.setup: Michael has just broken up with Maggie over the fake pregnancy. He walks out.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's timing is immediately catastrophic — he arrives to tell Michael he's going to be a father at the exact moment Michael has concluded there is no baby. The additional irony: Tobias announcing Michael's fatherhood rather than his own.
jokes.explanation: The timing is structurally perfect — the cliffhanger reversal arrives at maximum comedic impact after Michael's triumphant exit. Tobias delivering someone else's paternity news with the same joy as if it were his own is character-specific obliviousness. The episode ends on this revelation, maximizing rewatch value. 'You're gonna be a daddy!' as a curtain line for a sitcom cliffhanger is well-executed.

jokes.text: [On the Next] Barry: 'The fat girl in my Skip Church's case? Not fat. The attorney bought her a fat suit.' / 'I've got another case. Maggie is suing you for paternity.'
jokes.setup: The 'On the Next' segment teases next episode.
jokes.punchline: Barry pieces together that his fat plaintiff was wearing a fat suit bought by opposing counsel — and then immediately pivots to announce Maggie is now suing Michael for paternity.
jokes.explanation: The 'On the Next' teaser functions as its own comedy unit in Arrested Development, and this one does good work: tying together all the threads while adding a new escalation (paternity suit). Barry's dawning realization is well-paced.

jokes.text: [On the Next] Lucille prays: 'Take something else from us. Do whatever you have to to keep my boy from going to war.'
jokes.setup: Lucille has been converted to prayer by Ann. She prays for Buster's safety.
jokes.punchline: Her prayer is 'take something else' — offering God a bargain where he can harm them in other ways as long as Buster stays safe. The 'something else' will turn out to be Buster's hand.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most structurally brilliant moments. Lucille's prayer — 'take something else from us' — will be answered literally when Buster loses his hand to the seal. The joke only fully lands on rewatch, but first-time viewers still get the dark irony of Lucille negotiating with God while implicitly agreeing to further suffering. Her maternal devotion expressed as a cost-benefit negotiation with the divine is perfect Lucille. Exceptional foreshadowing comedy.

jokes.text: [On the Next] 'Loose seal! Watch out for loose seal!' / 'I don't care about Lucille! She lies!' / Narrator: 'And then a seal bites off his hand.'
jokes.setup: Someone shouts 'Loose seal! Watch out for loose seal!' Buster mishears 'Lucille' and responds emotionally.
jokes.punchline: The narrator flatly announces: 'And then a seal bites off his hand.' — followed immediately by Buster's scream cut to '...men' (the end of 'Amen' from Lucille's prayer).
jokes.explanation: The crown jewel of the episode and one of the great comedy construction feats in sitcom history. Every element was planted: the seal dealer wife, the released seals, Buster's avoidance of the ocean, Lucille's prayer to 'take something else,' and the Loose Seal/Lucille homophone. The narrator's matter-of-fact delivery of 'And then a seal bites off his hand' is the perfect anti-climax. The cut to '...men' from Lucille's prayer is a structural masterstroke — the prayer is answered in the same moment it ends. This is what a 10 looks like: every element converges, the joke has been building for episodes, and it still lands as a shock. The economy of the narrator's line is perfect.

tmdb_overview: Just as Michael's relationship with Sally starts to flourish, Maggie Lizer returns nine months pregnant, and Michael immediately assumes he's the father. George Michael attempts to break up with Ann, and Buster learns he's being deployed to Iraq.
insight: Buster's amputation crisis spawns 40 jokes in 22 minutes—character chaos at peak density.
